title: Research Question & Literature Review - Multiple Response

questions.question: Which features make a research question good for a student project? (Select all that apply)

options.A: It is specific and clearly scoped to one study.
options.B: It is answerable with measurable variables.
options.C: It is feasible given the time and resources of one semester.
options.D: It is so broad that it covers an entire field at once.
options.E: It has already been definitively answered in the literature.

questions.explanation: A good RQ is specific, answerable, novel-enough, and scoped to a feasible study. D (too broad) and E (already answered) are exactly the risks a good RQ avoids.

questions.question: Which are the four common citation mistakes described in the course? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Citing irrelevant literature that does not support the claim.
options.B: Overciting definitions instead of citing once.
options.C: Misattributing a finding to the wrong author.
options.D: Citing the citation rather than the original source.
options.E: Formatting the reference list before writing the body.

questions.explanation: The four mistakes are citing irrelevant literature, overciting definitions, misattributing, and citing the citation. E is just a workflow choice, not a citation mistake.

questions.question: Which signals help judge whether a source is scholarly? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Whether the work was peer reviewed.
options.B: The credentials and expertise of the author.
options.C: The reputation of the publication venue.
options.D: How many total page views the page has received.
options.E: Whether the headline sounds confident and persuasive.

questions.explanation: Scholarly status rests on peer review, author credentials, and venue. D (views) and E (tone) are popularity and style cues that non-scholarly sources share too.

questions.question: Where do good research topics typically come from? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Your own personal curiosity about a phenomenon.
options.B: A menu of topics offered by a supervisor.
options.C: Identified gaps in the existing literature.
options.D: Whichever topic has the most existing papers, regardless of fit.

questions.explanation: Topics come from personal curiosity, a supervisor menu, and gaps in the literature. D picks by sheer volume rather than fit or feasibility and is not a recommended source.

questions.question: What does a literature review accomplish for your project? (Select all that apply)

options.A: It shows what prior work is relevant to your question.
options.B: It identifies the gap your research question fills.
options.C: It explains why your research question matters.
options.D: It lists every paper you read in alphabetical order by author.
options.E: It replaces the need to state any research question at all.

questions.explanation: A review shows relevance, locates the gap, and motivates your RQ. D is the summary/author-ordered approach to avoid, and E is false because the review supports the RQ rather than replacing it.

questions.question: Which actions reflect goal-directed academic reading? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Skimming a paper to judge its relevance to your goal.
options.B: Deep-reading a section to follow a specific argument.
options.C: Taking notes of key concepts and where you read them.
options.D: Reading every paper cover to cover in publication order.

questions.explanation: Goal-directed reading means skimming for relevance, deep-reading for argument, and noting concepts plus their sources for later citation. D is the novel-style top-to-bottom reading the course contrasts against.

questions.question: Which behaviours violate academic integrity or the course GenAI policy? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Submitting fully GenAI-generated text as your own writing.
options.B: Presenting another author's words as your own without quotation.
options.C: Trusting GenAI-produced references without verifying them.
options.D: Using Zotero or Mendeley to manage your references.
options.E: Quoting a source properly with quotation marks and a citation.

questions.explanation: Passing off GenAI or others' words as your own is plagiarism, and GenAI references are often hallucinated, so they must be verified. D (reference managers) and E (proper quoting) are good, allowed practices.

questions.question: Which are recognised ways to find sources for a literature review? (Select all that apply)

options.C: Following citation chains from a relevant paper.
options.D: Copying a classmate's reference list without reading the sources.

questions.explanation: Library databases, Google Scholar, and citation chains are the recommended search routes. D skips reading and evaluating the sources yourself, which the course requires.

questions.question: When evaluating a paper BEFORE deep-reading it, which cues are useful? (Select all that apply)

options.E: The colour of the journal's website.

questions.explanation: Before committing to a full read you can scan the abstract, conclusions, citation count, and recency. E is a cosmetic feature that carries no information about quality or relevance.

questions.question: Which strategies help avoid the "summary effect" in a literature review? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Organising sources by topic or purpose rather than by author.
options.B: Grouping studies into sub-themes for comparison.
options.C: Framing the review as sides of an ongoing debate.
options.D: Writing one isolated summary paragraph per source in turn.

questions.explanation: Organising by topic, grouping into sub-themes, and structuring around debate sides all defeat the summary effect. D is the author-by-author summarising that causes it.
